headline: Michigan's ground game overwhelms UCLA in high-scoring Week 2 shootout
body: Michigan's defense proved far sturdier than the scoreboard suggested, holding UCLA to 28 points despite allowing 368 passing yards to Nico Iamaleava. The Wolverines' 23-point victory margin came on the back of a rushing attack that consistently moved the ball downfield when it mattered most. Brock's two rushing touchdowns set the tone early, establishing a commitment to ground control that UCLA's defense simply could not match. UCLA's offense stayed competitive in yards and efficiency—Iamaleava finished with four touchdowns and no interceptions—but the Bruins' two fumbles proved catastrophic in a game where field position and momentum swung decisively.

The real separation came in how each team converted opportunities into touchdowns. Michigan's QB Browne managed just 336 yards and two scores, yet the Wolverines still scored 51 points. That efficiency speaks to Michigan's discipline and execution in the red zone. UCLA, despite Iamaleava's stellar 30-of-46 passing performance, could not sustain drives when the turnovers hit. Harris caught five passes for 86 yards and a score, keeping UCLA's passing game functional, but the fumbles negated two possessions that might have narrowed the gap significantly.

Michigan's defensive line presence in the second half created a creeping disadvantage for UCLA that became insurmountable by the fourth quarter. The Bruins never recovered their momentum after the second turnover, and Michigan's balanced offensive approach—not relying on either Browne or Brock to carry the load alone—kept UCLA's front seven honest. This was a wire-to-wire defensive performance that allowed Browne to manage the game rather than win it outright.
turningPoint: UCLA's second fumble in the third quarter, occurring in Michigan territory, ended any realistic comeback pathway and allowed the Wolverines to extend their lead to 23 points with clear field position advantage.

unsungHero.name: Blackburn
unsungHero.why: Seven tackles set the game's defensive floor without a sack, but his consistency in run defense allowed Michigan's rushing game to flourish and forced UCLA into passing situations where mistakes became more likely.
